So you could use a very similar technique. It's this process is really for anything in a way. Yes. For any kind of ideas that you want to come up with, or for coming up with headlines, for coming up with bullets, you really start with, you really, you don't just go right into it. Like, give me 20 headlines, right? You're like, okay, here's the avatar. You create building blocks, and then you take those building blocks and you put those into the AI. And you say, okay, with these building blocks, now give me 10 headlines, 10 bullets. Yes. And make sure they're interesting, provocative. Compelling. Compelling. Yeah. With cliff hangers. With cliff hangers. Yeah. Yeah. And that's key. So a lot of people, I think now approach it as in just like that, they go, oh, I'll just ask you for 10 headlines. I'll just ask it for 15 subject lines on this topic. And the prompt is like a sentence or two. You could, like again, you could do that, but what you get back is often generic crappy copy that you can't really do much with, or you'll have to do it all day long to maybe get a couple of good ideas that you then have to write on your own. But the way this works with a process, especially with chat TBT, if you built up a body of interaction with it, where you have explored pains, problems, fears, desires, hopes, goals, dreams, features, benefits, outcomes, mechanisms, etc. What chat TBT does is that it's, I'll say remember, even though it doesn't actually remember, but it can look back about 8,000 words. And the more you have built up through prompts, the more it'll reference what you have built up. And so then when you get to the final stage of asking for 10 headlines for a sales page, and you've already talked about what the product is, you've already talked about what the avatar is, you've already explored all the negatives, all the positives, and you've already explored creative ideas and stories and all these things. When you then ask for 10 headlines or 20 headlines, it'll reference and make use of the history of what you've done, if you ask it to. And the headlines that it'll give you are far more specific, far more compelling and interesting and emotional with all that work done than if you were to just ask it cold. But this is also like how any copyrighter is. If I sit down to write a sales page one day and I start from scratch on day one, if I start writing 10 headlines on that day, like if my headlines will be shitty, like they'll be awful. Right. But two weeks later, after I've done the research, my headlines will be far better than if I did it on day one. Same idea applies here. You can go in cold, you can ask for things cold, and you'll get something. But what you get after interacting with it will be far better and far more useful if you just interact with it. And that interaction can happen over the course of an hour or less or longer, whatever. Yeah, it's there, it's available. So the same principles that would seem would apply to any sort of using AI for any sort of creativity or ideas. Like if I have an employee that is a problem, they're always coming in late, or they want to raise or whatever it is. Right. It's like you could, if you want to ask GPT, if you want to ask AI, what are some creative ideas for dealing with this problem? Right. You want to give it as much background as possible. You want to give it as much, you know, like, you know, I want ideas that, you know, that these are my goals. I want ideas that are good for the employee. I want ideas that will help the situation. I want ideas that won't get me in trouble with HR. You know, whatever it is. You want to be really specific and you want, you can't assume that AI understands the situation the way you would. If I asked you, I'm having trouble with an employee, what do you think I should do? You'll know, okay, you know, David wants this, he wants this is how this thing sort of thing usually works. It's a shared history. Yeah. It's a shared history. AI doesn't have that. Exactly. And the more you give it that, you know, like, let's say you want to come up with ideas for a new product, right? You just, you don't want to say, give me 10 ideas for a new product, right? You want to say, this is the market. This is the, this is what I'm trying to do. Do it in this specific area. It must be a mechanized thing. It must fit in a box this size, but you know, whatever it is. Exactly. And you can take any existing text. So these tools right now are text based. Eventually you'll be able to do this with video and audio as well, like where you can just give it like an audio file and then it'll produce stuff. But for now you take any text and you put it into the prompt and you just define it as text. And then there's a text. And then you, you can ask questions based on the text. And let's say you take, let's say you take the transcript of like a podcast where an HR professional is being interviewed by a business owner about how to deal with employees. And that's the topic of their podcast, like two hours long, the transcript, right? You take part of that transcript, you put it in and as a text and then you can ask, ask AI for like, based on this text above, what are the five things you should do if you're having a problem with an employee? What are the five things you shouldn't, should or shouldn't do? How, how, what's the best way to approach having a difficult conversation with an employee? And then based on that text, if those topics are in that text, then it'll find it and summarize it for you and give you that in, in those five bullet points that you asked for. And it'll do so with any text, like any text you have that's relevant to what you're doing and the questions you're asking, reviews from wherever, Amazon, somewhere else, texts from forums like Reddit or other forums, texts from social media, anything that can be turned into just simple text can be mined for all kinds of possibilities. You can ask it like in, in these, in these, in this collection of reviews, what stages of muscle hierarchy are present? And then it'll give you those stages that are present that people talk about. And you can ask what fears are the most prevalent and common in these reviews. And then it'll look for the most common and prevalent fears and then give it to you. And that's endless. Fears, problems, pains, hopes, dreams, desires, anything you can think to ask for, it'll find in that text if it's present and not just find it, but you can ask, it's like a rank it, like, which are the, you know, five most common fears, rank them from top to bottom in an ascending order or descending order. And it'll do so. You know, it's just, what's so interesting to me, if I just interrupt for just a second. Yeah, do it, interrupt me. Yeah. Is that you, you always, you break things down when you deal with AI. You don't just ask for everything at once. You say, what are the fears? What are the this? What are the that? You don't just say, look at this copy and look at this text that I've extracted and use it to tell me what to do in my situation. Right. You really ask it one thing at a time. You're so patient when it comes to this stuff. What are the fears? What are the hopes? What are the dreams? What are the, what's the language that this employee is using when they talk about it? Right. Yeah. Specific, specific, specific. And then you refeed it back in or AI remembers, if you're in the same session, and then utilizes all that. Yes. And the reason you're doing that is, is part practical and just part is better this way in general for your creativity also to see not everything all at once, but in the step by step. Because like I said, creativity is a process. But the reason you're doing them step by step is, but if you're asking for too many things and all the things all at once, your output is not going to be as good as if you're asking for things step by step. And why is that? Why does it not, it's AI. It's supposed to be super smart. Why can't it? Yeah. Well, it's just the way it's filtered and programmed to work. So what I found is a sweet spot that beyond 800 to 1000 words of an output coming back to me, the quality of what it tells me degrades. As it won't be as detailed, it won't be as interesting, and it'll start just becoming very generic. So when I asked for fears, I might ask for fears, problems, pains, because they're all negatives and there's synonyms to each other and they're very related to the negative emotions that I'm looking for. It'll give me stuff back, but it'll give me stuff back better than if I combine and say, what are all the fears, pains and problems, and then what are all the hope streams and desires? Because I'm keeping it one track minded. I don't want to combine the fears and the positives in one prompt, because then it'll give me them and it'll attempt to solve things for me before I have a chance to explore the fears, pains and problems. And it'll start connecting the dots too soon before I have a chance to review what would actually make sense. So I'll ask for all the negatives first and it'll give me those. And because I'm keeping it single track focused on just these types of fears and pains and problems, the output will be much better than if I started asking for more or different things. Then I'll ask for the positives and keep it single minded, so to speak, on that in the output that I get. Because if you do it that way, because of the way it's just the way it's coded to interact with you and the way the filters that has in place and the limitations they've set for itself, it could generate 10,000 words all the way go, but the quality will degrade. And so you will get 10,000 words, but 9,000 out of those words will be just pure dog shit usually, right? So just because of limitations, it's better for you to keep it single minded and focused per prompt so that you get the most out of the output every single time. And also, if you ask for 10 fears in a single prompt, you'll get 10 better expressed fears than if you ask for first give me 10 fears and then 10 hopes in the single prompt. If it then gives you 20 things in total, it won't be as expressive and detailed in the bullets as if you asked it for them one by one. So this is just a trial and error that I realized this from working with it for a couple of years. Like I realized, okay, there are some limitations to when output starts degrading and quality, and therefore I'm going to stay within those limitations to make sure that I don't get just garbled and crappy outputs.
